{{draft}} {{infobox deity | type = Shinto | cult_centre = {{ill|Awata Shrine|ja|粟田神社}}
[[Yasaka Shrine]]
{{ill|Katano-jinja|ja|片埜神社}}
{{ill|Suga-jinja|ja|須我神社}}
{{ill|Hyōsu-jinja|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}} }} [[ja:八島士奴美神]] {{AFC comment|1=See [[Draft:Yashimajinumi old]] for some info[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:35, 27 April 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|八島士奴美神}} (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god{{Cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == Shrines to worship == * [[Awata Shrine]] (Higashiyama-ku, [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City]]) * [[Yasaka Shrine]] (Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto-shi, Kyoto) * [[Otokoyama Shrine]] ([[Kagawa Prefecture]][[Sanuki, Kagawa]][[Sanuki City]][[Samukawa|Samukawa Town]]) * Katano Shrine ([[Osaka Prefecture]][[Hirakata|Hirakata City]] Makinosaka) * Sukha Shrine ([[Mie Prefecture]][[Matsusaka|Matsusaka City]] Ureshino Gongenmae Town) * Kawabe Shrine ([[Shimane Prefecture]][[Unnan|Unnan City]][[Kisuki, Shimane|Kizugi Town]]) * Suga Shrine (Unnan City, Shimane Prefecture][[Daitō, Shimane|Daitō Town]]) * Tabe-jinja Shrine ([[Izumo, Shimane]][[Sada, Shimane|Sada-cho]], Shimane Prefecture) * Hyōshūjinja Shrine ([[Hyōgo Prefecture]][[Nishiwaki, Hyōgo|Nishiwaki City]][[Kurodashō, Hyōgo|Kurodashō Town]]) * Yashima Te Shrine ([[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Tagata District, Shizuoka|Takata County]][[Kannami|Kannami Town]]) == See also == * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kushinadahime]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories |[[Category: Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] }}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{infobox deity | type = Shinto | cult_centre = {{ill|Awata Shrine|ja|粟田神社}}
[[Yasaka Shrine]]
{{ill|Katano-jinja|ja|片埜神社}}
{{ill|Suga-jinja|ja|須我神社}}
{{ill|Hyōsu-jinja|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}} | spouse = {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}} }} [[ja:八島士奴美神]] {{AFC comment|1=See [[Draft:Yashimajinumi old]] for some info[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:35, 27 April 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Yashimajinumi|ja|八島士奴美神}} (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan. He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |website=web.archive.org}}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}} and husband of {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto.. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi.. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kushinadahime]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories |[[Category: Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] }} {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |website=web.archive.org}}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto.. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi.. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son {{ill|Yashimajinumi|ja|八島士奴美神}}.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kushinadahime]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories |[[Category: Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] }} {{Gion cult}}

It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi.. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son {{ill|Yashimajinumi|ja|八島士奴美神}}.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kushinadahime]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] {{Gion cult}}

{{Short description|Japanese deity, son of Susanoo}} {{infobox deity | type = Shinto | cult_centre = {{ill|Awata Shrine|ja|粟田神社}}
[[Yasaka Shrine]]
{{ill|Katano-jinja|ja|片埜神社}}
{{ill|Suga-jinja|ja|須我神社}}
{{ill|Hyōsu-jinja|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}} | spouse = {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}} }} '''Yashimajinumi''' (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |website=web.archive.org}}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, {{Interlanguage link|Konohanachiruhime|ja|木花知流比売}}. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kushinadahime]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] {{Gion cult}}

{{ill|Suga-jinja|ja|須我神社}}
{{ill|Hyōsu-jinja|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}} | spouse = [[Konohanachiru-hime]] }} '''Yashimajinumi''' (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] {{Gion cult}}

He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[category:Japanese gods]] [[category:Izumo Province]] [[category:Gion cult]] [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[category:Japanese gods]] [[category:Izumo Province]] [[category:Gion cult]] [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[category:Japanese gods]] [[category:Izumo Province]] [[category:Gion cult]] [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[category:Japanese gods]] [[category:Izumo Province]] [[category:Gion cult]] [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[category:Japanese gods]] [[category:Izumo Province]] [[category:Gion cult]] [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]][[en:Yashimajinumi]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:dd]][[category:Yashimajinumi]][[category:dd]] [[category:1 revision imported]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:kk]][[en:Yashimajinumi]][[category:kk]] [[category:redirect]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yashimajinumi]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]][[en:Yashimajinumi]] {{Gion cult}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Short description|Japanese deity, son of Susanoo}} {{infobox deity | type = Shinto | cult_centre = {{ill|Awata Shrine|ja|粟田神社}}
[[Yasaka Shrine]]
{{ill|Katano Shrine|ja|片埜神社}}
{{ill|Suga-jinja|ja|須我神社}}
{{ill|Hyōzu Shrine|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu}} | spouse = [[Konohanachiru-hime]] }} '''Yashimajinumi''' (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] {{ill|Katano Shrine|ja|片埜神社}}
{{ill|Suga-jinja|ja|須我神社}}
{{ill|Hyōzu Shrine|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu}} | spouse = [[Konohanachiru-hime]] }} '''Yashimajinumi''' (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=draft}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]] ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Yashimajinumi]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]]

[[Yasaka Shrine]]

{{ill|Hyōzu Shrine|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)|12=draft}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=draft}} | spouse = [[Konohanachiru-hime]] }} '''Yashimajinumi''' (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=draft}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[sn:Yashimajinumi]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]]

These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Yashimajinumi]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]]

His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] }} [[sn:Yashimajinumi]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:八島士奴美神]] [[zh:八島士奴美神]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

[[:en:Suga Shrine|Suga-jinja]]
{{ill|Hyōzu Shrine|ja|兵主神社 (西脇市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōzu Shrine|qq=}} | father = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | mother = [[Kushinadahime]] | children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}} | spouse = [[Konohanachiru-hime]] }} '''Yashimajinumi''' (八島士奴美神) was a Japanese god.{{cite web |title=Yashimajinumi • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/yashimajinumi/ |access-date=2023-04-27 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. He appears in the [[Kojiki]], an old Japanese text. He is the first in a line of seventeen generations from Susanoo. The [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] has a different version of his lineage. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]]]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] }} [[sn:Yashimajinumi]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:八島士奴美神]] [[zh:八島士奴美神]]

His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]]]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] }} [[en:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]] [[ja:八島士奴美神]] [[zh:八島士奴美神]] {{wikidata link|Q55522907}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}} His name "Mighty Master Ruling Eightfold Isles" implies he ruled over all of Japan.{{rp|227}} He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]].{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}{{Cite web |last=Mori |first=Mizue |title=Yashimajinumi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9724 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}} and father of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}{{rp|278}} and husband of [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. He is part of the long line from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] to [[Ōkuninushi]]. He has other names in the Nihongi. These include Suga no yuyamanushi mina samoruhiko yashimajino and Suga no yuina saka karuhiko yashimade no mikoto. == Origin and Lineage == He is the son of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kushinadahime]]. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. It places [[Ōkuninushi]] as his descendant in the fifth generation. The Kojiki says Ōkuninushi is in the sixth generation. Yashimajinumi married Ōyamatsumi's daughter, [[Konohanachiru-hime]]. This marriage links him to Ōkuninushi. == Significance == Yashimajinumi's role is important in Japanese mythology. He connects various deities in these stories. His family ties show the relationships among the gods in ancient texts.. ==Shrines== He is worshipped at [[Yasaka Shrine]].{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto}} [[Suga Shrine]], claims to stand on the site of the palace Shinto deity [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] built after defeating the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], enshrines [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], [[Kushinadahime]], and their son Yashimajinumi.{{cite web |title=第十六番 須我神社 |url=https://shinbutsu.jp/shrines-temples/sugajinja/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=出雲國神仏霊場を巡る旅 (Izumo-no-kuni shinbutsu reijo o meguru tabi) |publisher=社寺縁座の会 (Shaji Enza no Kai)}} ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] ==References == {{reflist}} ]]]] {{Gion cult}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Izumo Province]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] }} [[en:Yashimajinumi]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yashimajinumi]] [[ja:八島士奴美神]] [[zh:八島士奴美神]] {{wikidata link|Q55522907}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
